perturbation.original_text: Each of PictureTel and Polycom shall (i) use the Confidential Information obtained from the other solely for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction and for no competitive or other purpose; (ii) not disclose the Confidential Information to any third party, except for disclosures to its directors, executive officers and representatives of its advisors (such as independent accountants, investment bankers, consultants and attorneys) acting on its behalf (collectively, its "Representatives") who in each case, in such party's reasonable judgment, need to know such information for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction and who are set forth on Annex A hereto, as it may be amended by the parties from time to time; (iii) inform its Representatives of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and direct its Representatives to treat the Confidential Information confidentially and in accordance with the terms of this Agreement; (iv) take all additional reasonable precautions necessary to prevent the disclosure of the Confidential Information by its Representatives to any third party; and (v) be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of PictureTel and Polycom shall (i) use the Confidential Information obtained from the other solely for the general purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction and considering other purposes; (ii) endeavor not to disclose the Confidential Information to any third party, except for disclosures to its directors, executive officers and representatives of its advisors (such as independent accountants, investment bankers, consultants and attorneys) acting on its behalf (collectively, its "Representatives") who in each case, in such party's good faith belief, may need to know such information for the purpose of loosely evaluating a possible Transaction and who are set forth on Annex A hereto, as it may be amended by the parties from time to time; (iii) generally inform its Representatives of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and suggest its Representatives treat the Confidential Information confidentially and generally in accordance with the terms of this Agreement; (iv) consider precautions to prevent the disclosure of the Confidential Information by its Representatives to any third party; and (v) be potentially responsible for any gross breach of this Agreement by its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces several ambiguities. 'General purpose' and 'considering other purposes' broadens the allowed use of confidential information. 'Endeavor not to disclose', 'good faith belief', 'may need to know', 'loosely evaluating', 'generally inform', 'suggest its Representatives', 'treat the Confidential Information confidentially and generally', 'consider precautions', and 'potentially responsible for any gross breach' weakens the obligations. These vague terms make enforcement difficult and contradict the original intent of strict confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act aims to protect trade secrets. The modifications weaken the confidentiality obligations, potentially enabling the misappropriation of trade secrets, which the original agreement seeks to prevent. The original agreement's strict language supports the prevention of actions that could violate the EEA, while the modified agreement's ambiguous language does not provide sufficient protection, thus contradicting the spirit and intent of the EEA.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract significantly weakens confidentiality obligations, potentially conflicting with the Economic Espionage Act's aim to protect trade secrets. The original contract mandated strict confidentiality, while the changed version uses ambiguous language like 'endeavor not to disclose,' 'good faith belief,' and 'potentially responsible for any gross breach.' These changes reduce the enforceability of the agreement and create loopholes, making it easier to misappropriate confidential information. Snippet 1 references the Economic Espionage Act of 1996, showing Congress trying to curtail corporate espionage.

perturbation.original_text: If either party is required (by interrogatories, requests for information or documents, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process) to disclose any Confidential Information, it is agreed that such party will provide the other party with prompt notice of such request so that such other party may seek an appropriate protective order and/or waive the notifying party's compliance with the provisions of this Agreement. If, in the absence of a protective order, either party is nonetheless compelled to disclose Confidential Information, such party may disclose, without liability hereunder, only that portion of the Confidential Information that such party is advised in writing by outside legal counsel is legally required to be disclosed; provided that such party gives to the other party written notice of the portion of the Confidential Information to be disclosed as far in advance of its disclosure as is practicable and, upon such other party's request, uses reasonable efforts to obtain assurances that confidential treatment will be accorded to such information.
perturbation.changed_text: If either party is requested (possibly by interrogatories, requests for information or documents, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process) to disclose some Confidential Information, it is generally agreed that such party might provide the other party with notice of such request so that such other party may consider seeking a protective order and/or consider waiving the notifying party's compliance with some of the provisions of this Agreement. If, in the absence of a protective order, either party feels obligated to disclose Confidential Information, such party may disclose, without assuming definite liability hereunder, some portion of the Confidential Information that such party feels advised in writing by outside legal counsel is somewhat required to be disclosed; provided that such party gives to the other party some notice of the portion of the Confidential Information to be disclosed reasonably in advance of its disclosure and, upon such other party's general request, uses efforts to seek assurances that confidential treatment will generally be accorded to such information.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity through the use of terms like 'possibly', 'some', 'generally agreed', 'might provide', 'consider seeking', 'some of the provisions', 'feels obligated', 'assuming definite liability', 'some portion', 'feels advised', 'somewhat required', 'some notice', 'reasonably in advance', 'general request', and 'generally be accorded'. These soften the mandatory obligations and create uncertainty about the level of compliance required, particularly regarding the notification and protective measures.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 26(c) outlines the process for obtaining protective orders to protect confidential information in discovery. The original text ensures the parties are promptly notified and can seek protective orders. The modified text weakens this obligation making it discretionary. This contradicts FRCP 26(c) because it introduces doubt about if proper, legally sound actions will be taken to protect the confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities that weaken the original obligation to protect confidential information, potentially contradicting the intent of FRCP 26(c). The original text mandates 'prompt notice' and efforts to obtain assurances of confidential treatment, while the changed text uses terms like 'possibly', 'some', 'generally agreed', 'might provide', and 'consider seeking'. These terms create uncertainty about the level of compliance required. While neither snippet explicitly states the requirement for 'prompt notice', the Law Explanation states that the original text ensures parties are promptly notified and can seek protective orders and that the modified text weakens this obligation making it discretionary. This weakening of the obligation is a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to indemnify the other party from any damages, loss, cost or liability (including, without limitation, legal fees and the cost of enforcing this Agreement) arising out of or resulting from any unauthorized use or disclosures of any Confidential Information by the other party or its Representatives. It is understood and agreed that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by either party and that the non-breaching party shall be entitled to seek equitable relief, including specific performance and injunction, as a remedy for any such breach. Each party agrees to waive, and to use its best efforts to cause its directors, officers, employees or agents to waive any requirement for the securing or posting of any bond in connection with such remedy. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by either party, but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity to the non-breaching party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party vaguely agrees to potentially indemnify the other party from alleged damages, some loss, possible cost or arguable liability (potentially including legal fees and the cost of vaguely enforcing this Agreement) arising out of or allegedly resulting from generally unauthorized use or somewhat confidential disclosures of some Confidential Information by the other party or its Representatives. It may be understood and agreed that money damages would not absolutely be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by either party and that the non-breaching party may be entitled to seek equitable relief, including specific performance and injunction, as a potential remedy for some such breach. Each party somewhat agrees to generally waive, and to generally use its efforts to cause its directors, officers, employees or agents to waive some requirement for the vague securing or posting of any bond in connection with such remedy. Such remedies shall not particularly be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by either party, but shall vaguely be in addition to all other remedies potentially available at law or in equity to the non-breaching party.
perturbation.explanation: The inclusion of terms such as 'vaguely agrees', 'potentially indemnify', 'alleged damages', 'some loss', 'possible cost', 'arguable liability', 'potentially including', 'vaguely enforcing', 'allegedly resulting', 'generally unauthorized', 'somewhat confidential', 'some Confidential Information', 'may be understood', 'not absolutely be', 'may be entitled', 'potential remedy', 'some such breach', 'somewhat agrees', 'generally waive', 'generally use its efforts', 'some requirement', 'vague securing', 'not particularly be deemed', and 'vaguely be in addition to' undermines the enforceability of the indemnification and remedies provisions. These terms create significant ambiguity about the parties' obligations and rights in case of a breach.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Contract Law - General Principles of Contract Interpretation
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts contract law emphasizes the importance of clear and unambiguous language in contracts. Ambiguous terms are construed against the drafter. The numerous vague terms added significantly undermine the clarity and enforceability of the indemnification and remedies provisions, contradicting the principles of contract interpretation under Massachusetts law. Chapter 93A is about liability and damages, which is made ambiguous through the changes.
perturbation.justification: The original contract established clear obligations related to indemnification, remedies, and waivers. The changed text introduces numerous ambiguities by adding terms like 'vaguely agrees,' 'potentially indemnify,' and 'somewhat confidential'. The Law Explanation states that Massachusetts contract law requires clear and unambiguous language, and ambiguous terms are construed against the drafter. The added vague terms undermine the clarity and enforceability of key provisions. The Perturbation Explanation details how each change weakens the contractual obligations. The absence of scraped law snippets does not change the fact that the alteration creates ambiguity which, according to the Law Explanation, is against legal principles, leading to a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party will keep a record of each person, other than its Representatives, that is afforded access to the other party and/or the Confidential Information of the other party, and before such access is afforded, each party agrees to inform such person of the requirements of this Agreement. Each party will furnish to the other party, upon request, a written copy of such record of such persons, other than its Representatives, that have been afforded access to the other party and/or the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party might keep a general record of each person, other than its Representatives, that may be afforded incidental access to the other party and/or possibly some Confidential Information of the other party, and before such access is potentially afforded, each party generally agrees to mostly inform such person of some of the requirements of this Agreement. Each party may furnish to the other party, upon occasional request, a general copy of such general record of such persons, other than its Representatives, that may have been afforded incidental access to the other party and/or possibly some Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The changes introduce several ambiguities: Replacing 'will keep a record' with 'might keep a general record' makes the record-keeping discretionary. 'May be afforded incidental access' and 'possibly some Confidential Information' weakens the tracking requirement. 'Before such access is potentially afforded' is vague. 'Generally agrees to mostly inform such person of some of the requirements' suggests incomplete disclosure of the agreement terms. 'May furnish' and 'upon occasional request' makes providing the record optional. All these phrases significantly reduce the obligation to track and control access to confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 404 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX) requires companies to establish and maintain internal controls over financial reporting. While this agreement concerns confidentiality, the handling of confidential information can impact financial reporting (e.g., if it relates to future earnings, pending deals, etc.). By weakening the requirements for tracking who has access to confidential information, the modified text creates a situation where the company's internal controls are less effective. This could contradict the requirements of SOX Section 404 because the company may not be able to adequately demonstrate that it has controls in place to prevent the misuse or unauthorized disclosure of confidential financial information. Especially in the event the information could be material to an audit.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Final Rule: Management's Report on Internal Control Over Financial Reporting and Certification of Disclosure in Exchange Act Periodic Reports; Rel. No. 33-8238 Home | Previous Page Final Rule: Management's Report on Internal Control Over Financial Reporting and Certification of Disclosure in Exchange Act Periodic Reports SECURITIES AND EXCHANGE COMMISSION 17 CFR PARTS 210, 228, 229, 240, 249, 270 and 274 [RELEASE NOS. 33-8238; 34-47986; IC-26068; File Nos. S7-40-02; S7-06-03] RIN 3235-AI66 and 3235-AI79 MANAGEMENT'S REPORT ON INTERNAL CONTROL OVER FINANCIAL REPORTING AND CERTIFICATION OF DISCLOSURE IN EXCHANGE ACT PERIODIC REPORTS AGENCY: Securities and Exchange Commission. ACTION: Final rule. SUMMARY: As directed by Section 404 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002, we are adopting rules requiring companies subject to the reporting requirements of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, other than registered investment companies, to include in their annual reports a report of management on the company's internal control over financial reporting. The internal control report must include: a statement of management's responsibility for establishing and maintaining adequate internal control over financial reporting for the company; management's assessment of the effectiveness of the company's internal control over financial reporting as of the end of the company's most recent fiscal year; a statement identifying the framework used by management to evaluate the effectiveness of the company's internal control over financial reporting; and a statement that the registered public accounting firm that audited the company's financial statements included in the annual report has issued an attestation report on management's assessment of the company's internal control over financial reporting. Under the new rules, a company is required to file the registered public accounting firm's attestation report as part of the annual report. Furthermore, we are adding a requirement that management evaluate any change in the company's internal control over financial reporting that occurred during a fiscal quarter that has materially affected, or is reasonably likely to materially affect, the company's internal control over financial reporting. Finally, we are adopting amendments to our rules and forms under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and the Investment Company Act of 1940 to revise the Section 302 certification requirements and to require issuers to provide the certifications required by Sections 302 and 906 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 as exhibits to certain periodic reports. perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity that weakens the obligation to track and control access to confidential information, potentially impacting internal controls over financial reporting as required by Section 404 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX). The original contract used definitive language like 'will keep a record' and 'agrees to inform,' while the modified version uses ambiguous language like 'might keep a general record,' 'may be afforded incidental access,' and 'generally agrees to mostly inform,' making the requirements discretionary. The LLM explanation highlights that weakening these requirements could make the company's internal controls less effective, potentially contradicting SOX Section 404 if the company cannot adequately demonstrate controls to prevent misuse or unauthorized disclosure of confidential financial information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions Section 404 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act, stating that companies must include in their annual reports 'a report of management on the company's internal control over financial reporting'. The ambiguous changes in the modified contract compromise the effectiveness of the internal control, leading to a legal contradiction.
